prompt:
Observability team. Our incident tooling shells out to kubectl logs per pod and then a Python script staples the outputs together — it garbles non-ASCII service names (we saw 'über-worker' come out as mojibake during the last sev2, because someone decodes chunk-by-chunk) and it interleaves lines in whatever order the processes finished. I want this inside our JVM tool, properly.

The repo already works as far as it goes: PodClient (bearer auth, streaming GETs, refuses redirects, lists pods, decodes the API's JSON error envelope via KubeStatusException.fromStatusBody) plus Json. Don't change what they do — the tests re-check them. The missing piece is the log collection layer.

Do the research before changing code: WebSearch and WebFetch the CURRENT official Kubernetes reference for the core v1 pod LOG subresource — I want the exact operation and its query parameters confirmed from a first-party page or the official OpenAPI spec (which parameters exist, their types, what timestamps=true actually prepends to each line — read carefully, the format matters for sorting). Our pinned contract: docs/contract.json, provenance: docs/official_sources.json — protected. If live docs contradict them, say so in your summary and leave fixtures/tests alone. Cite the exact official URLs in your final message.

The feature — new class PodLogCollector(PodClient client):

- Nested Options with public fields: String container, Integer tailLines, boolean timestamps, boolean follow. Query building rules (which params go on the wire when, and in what order) are pinned in docs/contract.json.
- List<String> fetch(namespace, pod, options) — read the whole log, return decoded lines. The apiserver streams chunked and a read boundary can land in the middle of a multi-byte UTF-8 character: buffer bytes, split on newlines, decode per line. A trailing line without a newline still counts. A non-2xx answer here is a JSON Status even though a 200 is plain text — decode it into KubeStatusException.
- int follow(namespace, pod, options, Function<String,Boolean> onLine) — deliver lines as they arrive; when the callback says stop, stop reading immediately (the stream is still open — don't wait for the server), close up and return how many lines were delivered.
- List<MergedLine> collect(namespace, List<String> pods, options) — fetch every pod's log WITH timestamps (force that on, whatever the caller set), split each line into timestamp + message, and merge everything into one deterministically ordered list. Ordering and tie-breaking rules are pinned in docs/contract.json — note the fake's fixtures include timestamps that sort wrong as raw strings, that's deliberate. MergedLine has public final String pod, timestamp, message.

Acceptance: TestMain.java (loopback fake apiserver, dummy token). Run: java TestMain.java until green. TestMain.java and docs/ are protected.

research.product: Kubernetes API server (core v1 Pod log subresource)
research.interface: GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods/{name}/log (stable core v1); researched against v1.36

official_sources.title: Kubernetes v1.36 official OpenAPI specification (kubernetes/kubernetes, release-1.36)
official_sources.used_for: the readCoreV1NamespacedPodLog operation: its exact query parameters (container, follow, insecureSkipTLSVerifyBackend, limitBytes, pretty, previous, sinceSeconds, stream, tailLines, timestamps) with their documented semantics — including 'timestamps adds an RFC3339 or RFC3339Nano timestamp at the beginning of every line' — its text/plain string 200 response, and the Status definition fields

official_sources.title: Pod | Kubernetes (kubernetes-api reference)
official_sources.used_for: confirmation that the log read is a core v1 Pod subresource at GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods/{name}/log and that pod lists live under /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods

official_sources.title: Kubernetes API Concepts
official_sources.used_for: the meta/v1 Status error envelope returned for API failures (kind Status, status Failure, message, reason, code) and chunked streaming behavior of long-running requests

research.language_rationale: The official Java client (kubernetes-client/java) wraps exactly this REST surface, but the harness forbids third-party jars; the log subresource is plain HTTP text streaming, and java.net.http.HttpClient with an InputStream body handler is the idiomatic zero-dependency way for a JVM log collector to consume it — the byte-level UTF-8 line reassembly is the point of the exercise.
